concise_definition: Continuity offense : Basketball offensive system which incorporates specific patterns of cuts, screens, passes, or dribble actions executed in a continuous manner on both sides of the court to generate the highest quality scoring opportunity near the basket or from the perimeter.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text:
Continuity offense is typically a team-oriented scheme that could potentially give all five players an opportunity to perform at least one action within the entire system at any given time.

Additionally, because each player could possibly carry out a specific action within the overall structure of the offense, that could help build skills as well as confidence.

One particular advantage of a continuity offense is that it is generally easier to for coaches to teach it, mainly due to the built-in routines. That could also be highly useful for youth basketball teams or inexperienced teams in general.

Table of Contents

- What is a continuity offense in basketball?

- What is an example of 3 out 2 in continuity offense?

- What is an example of 4 out 1 in continuity offense?

- What is an example of 5 out continuity offense?

- What is an example of Flex continuity offense?

- What is an example of Princeton chin continuity offense?

- What are possible disadvantages of a continuity offense?

sections.heading: What is a continuity offense in basketball?
sections.text:
A continuity offense is a system in basketball that incorporates specific patterns of actions such as cuts, screens, passes, or dribbles executed in a consecutive manner, especially on both sides of the floor.

The primary purpose of the continuity pattern is to ultimately produce the highest quality scoring opportunity near the basket or around the perimeter areas of the court.

sections.heading: How to understand the player roles and diagrams on this page
sections.text:
In reference to any diagrams below, each of the offensive players are generally represented by the standardbasketball positions, unless otherwise noted.

Essentially, 1 is thepoint guard; 2 is theshooting guard; 3 is thesmall forward; 4 is thepower forward; 5 is thecenter.

What’s more, in terms of a brief legend for the diagrams:

- Black Dot or Small Circle:The black dot or small circle represents the basketball.

- Solid Arrow:The solid arrow indicates movement or a cut on the basketball court.

- Dotted Arrow:The dotted arrow represents a basketball pass.

- Wavy Arrow:The wavy arrow indicates dribbling.

- T-Shaped Symbol:The T-shaped symbol signifies a screen, also known as a pick.

- Double pipe symbol:The double pipe symbol denotes handoff action.

sections.heading: What is an example of 3 out 2 in continuity offense?
sections.text: This is an example of3 out 2 in continuity offensethat includespass and screen awayaction alongside a simplecross screen, which could result in a scoring opportunity around the perimeter or near the rim.

sections.heading: Part 1: Cross screen and away screen
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of cross screen and away screen action within part 1 of a 3 out 2 in continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of cross screen and away screen action within part 1 of a 3 out 2 in continuity offense.]

To start, 3 receives the ball from 1 and as that happens, 5 cuts to the right side low post area via the cross screen set by 4. Moreover, 2 cuts to the top via the away screen set by 1.

Afterwards, 5 or 2 could receive possession of the ball from 3, which could lead to scoring possibilities via a low post move or three-point shot, respectively.

What’s more, 1 and 4 could replace at the left side wing and low post areas, respectively, following the screening actions.

sections.heading: Part 2: Cross and away continuity
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of cross screen and away screen action executed on the opposite side of the court within part 2 of a 3 out 2 in continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of cross screen and away screen action executed on the opposite side of the court within part 2 of a 3 out 2 in continuity offense.]

If 2 gains possession of the ball but is not open to take the three-point shot, then it is viable to implement the simple continuity pattern of the play.

So, 1 receives the ball from 2 and as that occurs, 5 cuts to the left side low post area via the cross screen of 4. Also, 3 cuts to the top via the away screen by 2.

3 or 5 could receive the ball from 1, which could result in opportunities to score points near the rim or around the perimeter.

sections.heading: What is an example of 4 out 1 in continuity offense?
sections.text: This is an example of 4 out 1 in continuity offense that features a initialdribble handoffand weak side exchange as well as a back screen, flare screen, and skip pass action on both sides of the floor.

sections.heading: Part 1: Dribble handoff and exchange
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of dribble handoff and exchange action within part 1 of a 4 out 1 in continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of dribble handoff and exchange action within part 1 of a 4 out 1 in continuity offense.]

To begin, 1 performs a dribble handoff alongside 3 near the right side wing. Afterwards, 3 dribbles back towards the right slot while 1 occupies the vacant right side wing.

Also, while that dribble action happens, 2 and 4 exchange slot and wing spots on the weak side, mainly to occupy their defenders.

sections.heading: Part 2: Back screen
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of a back screen within part 2 of a 4 out 1 in continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of a back screen within part 2 of a 4 out 1 in continuity offense.]

Next, 2 receives the ball from 1 and then 4 receives it from 2. As that happens, 1 cuts to the basket via the angled back screen set by 5.

If 1 is open after the cut, then 1 could receive the ball from 4 and score via a layup.

Otherwise, 1 could move out the lane and occupy the left side low post area for the time being. 5 could also fill the vacant right side wing as well following the screening action.

sections.heading: Part 3: Flare screen and back screen
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of a flare screen and back screen within part 3 of a 4 out 1 in continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of a flare screen and back screen within part 3 of a 4 out 1 in continuity offense.]

Next, 2 receives the ball again from 4 and as that occurs, 2 cuts to the right side wing via the flare screen set by 5.

From there, 3 could receive the ball from 2 and take the open three-point shot. However, if the shot is not available, then 4 could cut to the basket via the back screen set by 1, receive the ball from 3, and score around the basket.

If that is also not open, then 4 simply fills the adjacent right side low post area while 1 occupies the left side wing and 5 replaces at the right slot area, respectively.

sections.heading: Part 4: Flare screen and back screen continuity
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of flare screen and back screen action executed on the other side of the floor within part 4 of a 4 out 1 in continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of flare screen and back screen action executed on the other side of the floor within part 4 of a 4 out 1 in continuity offense.]

It is possible to execute the continuity pattern at this point. In essence, 5 receives the ball from 3 and as that occurs, 2 cuts to the left side wing via the flare screen set by 1.

Afterwards, 2 could gain possession of the ball via the skip pass thrown by 5 and take the open three-point shot.

However, if the shot is not available, then 3 could cut to the basket via the back screen set by 4, receive the ball from 2, and score via a layup or dunk.

If that option is also not feasible, then 3 could fill the left side low post area while 4 and 1 occupy the right side wing and left slot areas, respectively.

From that point, the continuity pattern could be executed once again on the other side of the floor.

sections.heading: What is an example of 5 out continuity offense?
sections.text: This is an example of5 out continuity offensebased on the pass and cut series. In essence, each player, initially stationed near one of five perimeter areas of the court, will pass the ball to a teammate and cut to the basket, as the name implies.

sections.heading: Part 1: Top to wing pass and cut
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of top to wing pass and cut action within part 1 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of top to wing pass and cut action within part 1 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

To start the play, 2 receives the ball from 1, represented by the dotted arrow. Following that, 1 immediately cuts to the basket and as that happens, 3 fills the vacant space at the top while 5 replaces at the left side vacant wing.

As that happens, if 1 is open, then 1 could receive the ball again from 2, which could then result in alayupat the rim.

Otherwise, if 1 is not open, then 1 could simply cut through to the empty left side corner that was recently vacated by 5.

sections.heading: Part 2: Wing to corner pass and cut
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of wing to corner pass and cut action within part 2 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of wing to corner pass and cut action within part 2 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

Next, 4 could receive the ball from 2 and cut to the basket. While that happens, 3 could fill in the space left by 2 at the right side wing, 5 could fill the top, and 1 could fill/replace at the left side wing.

If 2 is open, then 2 could receive the ball again from 4 and score via a layup. Otherwise, 2 simply cuts through to the left side corner recently vacated by 1.

sections.heading: Part 3: Corner to wing pass and cut
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of corner to wing pass and cut action within part 3 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of corner to wing pass and cut action within part 3 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

Next, 3 could receive the ball from 4, which is then followed up by 4 cutting to the basket from the corner.

If 4 is open, then 4 could receive the ball again from 3 and score via a layup at the rim. Otherwise, 4 simply self-replaces at the right corner area.

sections.heading: Part 4: Wing to top pass and cut
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of wing to top pass and cut action within part 4 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of wing to top pass and cut action within part 4 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

Continuing with the main action, 5 could receive possession of the ball from 3 who could cut to the basket from the right side wing.

As that occurs, 4 could also fill and replace at the emptied right side wing area. From there, 3 could receive the ball again from 5 and score a layup at the rim.

If 3 is not open, however, then 3 could fill and replace at the right side corner that was recently vacated by 4.

sections.heading: Part 5: Top to wing continuity
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of top to wing action executed on the other side of the floor within part 5 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of top to wing action executed on the other side of the floor within part 5 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

At this point, it is possible to execute the continuity pattern on the other side of the floor.

So, 1 receives the ball from 5. Afterwards, 5 cuts to the basket and could receive the ball again from 1, leading to a layup.

Additionally, 4 and 3 fill the top and right side wing, respectively. If 5 is not open, then 5 cuts to the empty right side corner.

sections.heading: Part 6: Wing to corner continuity
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of wing to corner action ran on the opposite side of the floor within part 6 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of wing to corner action ran on the opposite side of the floor within part 6 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

Next, 2 receives the ball from 1 who cuts to the basket thereafter. While that happens, 4, 3, and 5 fill the left side wing, top, and right side wing, respectively.

As that happens, 1 could receive the ball again and score via layup at the basket. Otherwise, 1 continues to cut through to the empty right side corner.

sections.heading: Part 7: Corner to wing continuity
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of corner to wing action carried out on the other side of the floor within part 7 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of corner to wing action carried out on the other side of the floor within part 7 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

Following that, 4 could receive the ball from 2, who then cuts to the basket. If that is open, then 2 could receive the ball from 4 and score via a layup.

If that is not open, then 2 simply self-replaces at the left side corner.

sections.heading: Part 8: Wing to top continuity
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of wing to top action applied on the opposite side of the court within part 8 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of wing to top action applied on the opposite side of the court within part 8 of a 5 out continuity offense.]

Next up, 3 could receive the ball from 4, who subsequently cuts to the rim. As that happens, 2 fills the empty space within the left side wing.

From there, 4 could receive the ball again from 3 and score around the basket via the layup. If that is not open, then 4 fills and replaces at the left side corner area.

Afterwards, the continuity could get carried out once again with 5 receiving the ball from 3, who would cut to the rim and so forth.

sections.heading: What is an example of Flex continuity offense?
sections.text: This is an example of Flex continuity offense, which comprises theflex screenanddown screenaction.

sections.heading: Part 1: Flex and down screen
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of flex and down screen action within part 1 of the Flex continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of flex and down screen action within part 1 of the Flex continuity offense.]

To start, 2 receives the ball from 1 and afterwards, 3 cuts to the basket via the flex screen set by 5. Also following that, 5 cuts up to the right slot via the down screen set by 1.

From there, 3 could receive the ball from 2 as the first scoring option in terms of attempting a layup.

If that is not available, then 5 could receive the ball instead and potentially take the open three-point shot as the second scoring option.

Otherwise, 5 could simply keep possession of the ball while 3 fills the left side low post area while 1 occupies the right side corner after the screening action.

sections.heading: Part 2: Flex and down screen continuity
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of flex and down screen action executed on the other side of the floor within part 2 of a Flex continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of flex and down screen action executed on the other side of the floor within part 2 of a Flex continuity offense.]

Next, it is possible to execute the continuity pattern on the other side of the floor.

Essentially, 4 cuts to the basket via the flex screen set by 3. Afterwards, 3 could also cut towards the left slot area via the down screen by 2.

Following that, 4 could receive the ball as the first scoring option by way of a layup near the rim.

If that is not open, then 3 could receive it instead and take the open three-point shot as the second scoring option.

Otherwise, 4 could retain the ball and the continuity could be performed again as 2 fills the left side corner and 4 occupies the right side low post area.

sections.heading: What is an example of Princeton chin continuity offense?
sections.text: This is an example of a continuity offense based onPrinceton chinthat demonstratesback screensandflare screens, which could lead to different scoring options.

sections.heading: Part 1: Back screen
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of a back screen within part 1 of a continuity offense that is based on Princeton chin series.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of a back screen within part 1 of a continuity offense that is based on Princeton chin series.]

To start, 2 receives the ball from 1 and then 4 receives it from 2. Afterwards, 1 cuts to the basket via the back screen set by 5 and could receive the ball from 4, resulting in a possible scoring opportunity.

Otherwise, 4 retains possession of the ball while 1 cuts through to the left side corner and 5 cuts to the middle of the high post after setting the screen.

sections.heading: Part 2: Double flare
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of double flare screens within part 2 of a continuity offense that is based on Princeton chin series.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of double flare screens within part 2 of a continuity offense that is based on Princeton chin series.]

Next, 2 could cut to the right side wing area by way of double flare screens set by 5 and 3.

Following that, 2 could receive the ball from 4 viaskip passand take the open three-point shot.

Alternatively, 2 could also dribble towards the basket, indicated by the wavy arrow, ultimately to score points themselves or even throw akick passwith 1 as the recipient.

Otherwise, if 2 is not open to receive the ball on the right side wing, then 4 could simply dribble towards the left slot while 1 fills behind at the left side wing area.

Additionally, 5 could cut back to the left side elbow area while 3 fills the right slot area.

sections.heading: Part 3: Back screen continuity
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of back screen action ran on the opposite side of the court within part 3 of a continuity offense that is based on Princeton chin series.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of back screen action ran on the opposite side of the court within part 3 of a continuity offense that is based on Princeton chin series.]

At this point, it’s possible to execute the continuity pattern on the other side of the floor.

Therefore, 3 receives the ball from 4 and 2 receives it from 3. Next, 4 cuts to the basket via the back screen set by 5.

After that, 4 could receive the ball from 2 and score via a layup if that is open.

Otherwise, 4 cuts through to the right side corner while 5 moves to the middle of the high post.

sections.heading: Part 4: Double flare continuity
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of double flare screens ran on the opposite side within part 4 of a continuity offense that is based on Princeton chin series.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of double flare screens ran on the opposite side within part 4 of a continuity offense that is based on Princeton chin series.]

Next, 3 could cut to the left side wing area via the double flare screens set by 5 and 1.

Afterwards, 3 could receive the ball from 2 by way of a skip pass and take the open three-point shot. Alternatively, 3 could attack the rim to score or throw the kick pass with 4 as the target.

Otherwise, if 3 is not open to receive the ball, then 2 could dribble towards the right slot while 4 fills behind at the right side wing area.

In addition to that, 5 could cut back to the right side elbow area while 1 fills the left slot area.

At this point, the continuity pattern could be ran on the other side of the floor again.

sections.heading: What are possible disadvantages of a continuity offense?
sections.text: Some possible disadvantages of a continuity offense is that it could be potentially ineffective against certain types of defense and it may not be as adequate when a team is losing the game, especially towards the end.

sections.heading: Potentially ineffective against certain types of defense
sections.text:
Essentially, the defensive team could counter the various cuts, screens, passes, or dribble actions within continuity offenses with strategies or schemes of their own.

For example, one of the primary scoring options within the 5 out offense is the pass and cut series.

Within this same series, a player will pass the ball to an adjacent teammate and then cut to the basket for possible scoring opportunities.

This particular strategy could be very useful against theman to man defensive system.

However, if the opposing team implements azone defensestrategy such as the 2-3 zone, then the pass and cut series may not be as useful.

It is because the2-3 zone defensefeatures three defenders near the basket. Therefore, if an offensive player were to cut to the basket via the 5 out pass and cut series, that same player might have challenges scoring around the basket.

sections.heading: Possibly inadequate when losing towards the end
sections.text:
The general premise of a continuity offense is to execute the various patterns over and over until the best available scoring opportunity becomes available.

This can become a potential problem, particularly as time winds down when the team is losing by a few points or more. The offensive team would essentially have to take quicker shots that may be of a lower quality, which goes against the overall patience factor of the continuity offense.
